coredata.dc:title: The up-converted photoluminescence and cell imaging of water-soluble carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have been successfully synthesized by green-one-pot hydrothermal method. The CDs with small particle size exhibit fairly good water solubility, tunable photoluminescence, up-converted photoluminescence, and low toxicity. What is more, the as-prepared CDs were successfully applied in the field of cell image.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structures of the samples. (a) XRD pattern of the samples in different pH, (b) TEM image, inset: lattice spacing, (c) particle size distribution and (d) SAED pattern of CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XPS spectrum, (c) C 1s and (d) O 1s spectra of the as-prepared CDs.
The PL spectra of the CDs in different pH value.
The up-converted photoluminescence spectra of the CDs (pH 7) (a) up-converted photoluminescence spectra and (b) normalized up-converted photoluminescence spectra.
(a) Cell viability, S180 sarcoma cells at the excitation wavelength of (b) 364 and (c) 488nm.

head.ce:title: The up-converted photoluminescence and cell imaging of water-soluble carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Key Laboratory for Magnetism and Magnetic Materials of Education, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, Xi’An University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have been successfully synthesized by green-one-pot hydrothermal method. The CDs with small particle size exhibit fairly good water solubility, tunable photoluminescence, up-converted photoluminescence, and low toxicity. What is more, the as-prepared CDs were successfully applied in the field of cell image.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon materials with their unique properties of environmentally friendly [1], non-toxic, and low cost have drawn attention to lots of researchers, such as carbon nanowires, carbon nanotubes or grapheme etc. [2,3]. Especially carbon quantum dots, as a new carbon nanomaterial with excitation-dependent photoluminescence and up-converted photoluminescence behavior, has attracted increasing attention because of their wide potential applications in photocatalysis [4], solar cell [5], bioimaging [6–8] and biomedicine [9–11]. As zero dimensional (0D) quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters less than 10nm [12,13], carbon dots are emerging as viable alternative to semiconductor quantum dots owing to their important photoluminescent properties, include cytotoxicity and excellent bioconjugate properties [14–18]. In view of wide variety of applications, lots of researches have been done, since the carbon dots were first reported in 2004 [13]. So far, a variety of methods have been developed to prepare fluorescent CDs, such as arc-discharge, laser ablation [19], electrochemical oxidation [20], and microwave heating [21,22], etc. However, most of these methods involve expensive carbon sources, complex reaction and time-consuming processes, post-treatment processes, which limit the wide applications of CDs.

In this letter, we report that the water-soluble fluorescent CDs can be readily prepared by a simple one-step-green hydrothermal method using low-cost sucrose as carbon source. The obtained CDs exhibit excellent fairly good water-solubility, tunable photoluminescence, up-converted photoluminescence and the photoluminescence behavior of carbon quantum dots is investigated. What is more, the as-prepared CDs have been successfully applied in cell images.

The normal edible sucrose purchased from local supermarket was used as carbon source. The CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of sucrose in deionized (DI) water [23]. Briefly, 0.5g of sucrose was dispersed into 20mL DI water under magnetic stirring and ultrasound sonicated for 10min. Then the mixture was transferred into a 40mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C with different pH for 5h. After the completion of the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. A dark brown solution with precipitate was obtained. The solution was filtered to separate the precipitate, and the brown filtrate was centrifuged at high speed (15000rpm). The resultant supernatant, containing fluorescent CDs, was dialyzed against DI water for 48h to remove impurities, such as inorganic ions and molecules. The CDs prepared in different pH had been obtained.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed by using the MTT assay. S180 cells (1×104cells/well) were grown in RPMI-1640 medium in a 96-well plate at 37°C and under 5% CO2 atmosphere, supplemented with calf serum (10%) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin in a fully humidified incubator. Then, the CDs with a concentration of 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320 and 640μg/mL were added to cell dishes, and these cell dishes were put into incubators at 37°C for 12h. After incubation for a defined time, the culture medium was removed and 20μL of MTT reagent (diluted in culture medium, 0.5mg/mL) was added, followed by incubating for another 2h. The MTT/medium was removed carefully and DMSO (150μL) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. Absorbance of the colored solution was measured at 570nm using a microplate reader.

After confirming the fluorescence from CDs and no distinct autofluorescence from the cell itself under similar conditions, the cellular image was obtained with a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, ZEISS, LSM 510 Meta, Germany). S180 cells (6×104cells/well) were seeded on a 6-well plate at 37°C for 24h. After that, the CDs solution with a concentration of 200μg/mL was added to the cell dishes. After a further 2h incubation, these CDs-loaded cells were washed with PBS three times to remove the free CDs attached on the outer surface of cell membrane. Cell luminescence imaging was detected on LSCM under excitation wavelength of 364nm and 488nm.

The structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (Rigaku D/MAX-2400). The morphologies and crystallography of the samples were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai-G2 F30). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by an ESCALAB-250. The PL spectra (photoluminescence and up-converted photoluminescence) were measured at room temperature with FLs920 steadystate/transientstate spectro xsort with xenon lamp in water solution and the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured by a Thermo Nicolet Nexus FTIR model 670 spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique.

Figure 1a shows the powder XRD pattern of the samples prepared in different pH. The diffraction peak of the CDs located at 2θ =22° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of carbons. The calculated grain sizes of the samples using Scherrer formula are around 5nm. No other characteristic diffraction peak of carbon is present, which could be attributed to the low crystallinity of the carbon dots.

Figure 1b is the TEM image of the typical sample which shows that the obtained CDs exhibit fairly good monodispersity and homogeneous distribution. The inset of Figure 1b is the HRTEM image of the CDs and the lattices plane can be clearly identified with an average spacing of 0.317nm, which is very close to the interplanar spacing of the diamond-like structure reported by Liu et al. [24]. From the particle size distribution histogram (Figure 1c), the particles are mostly of spherical shape with a narrow particles size distribution between 2 and 6nm (with an average diameter of 4.4nm). The distinct rings of the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Figure 1d) indicate the sample is polycrystalline.

The component and surface states of CDs were characterized by FTIR and XPS. Figure 2a is the FTIR spectrum of the carbon dots, which shows that the CDs exhibit characteristic absorption bands of OH at 3405cm−1 and CH stretching vibrations at 2936cm−1, 2880cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1640cm−1 and 1380cm−1. These results imply that the functional groups, including carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy groups and amino groups, were introduced onto the surfaces of the CDs during the reaction of the CDs. The XPS are carried out to further investigate the composition and surface state stretching vibrations. Figure 2b is the full XPS spectrum of carbon dots, which indicates the presence of carbon (C1s) and oxygen (O1s). In detail, the high resolution scan of C1s (Figure 2c) shows four peaks at 283.8, 285.4, 287.2 and 288.9eV, which correspond to CC, CO, CO and COOH, respectively. Moreover, the O1s spectrum (Figure 2d) shows two peaks at 530.9 and 532.5eV, which could be attributed to CO and CO, respectively. With those functional groups, the obtained CDs exhibit excellent water-solubility and are expected to be applied in biological imaging.

The PL spectra were performed to further investigate the fluorescent properties of the obtained CDs in different pH value. As shown in Figure 3 , all the samples exhibit excellent photoluminescence which show the obviously excitation-dependent property. As seen, the emission wavelength of sample (prepared in pH 7) increases from 432nm to 560nm with the excitation wavelength increases from 320nm to 500nm, what is more, which has a red shift compared with the samples prepare in pH 1 and 14 [25]. These unique fluorescent properties indicate that the same CDs could get different images and which may be the potential advantages in cell imaging. Further study shows that the sample prepared at pH 7 has a remarkable photoluminescence quantum yield of 8.9%, which is superior to the samples prepared in pH 1 (5.1%) and in pH 14 (7%).

The sample prepared in pH 7 was further studied in up-converted photoluminescence. Figure 4 is the spectrum of the CDs which shows the CDs exhibit obviously excitation-dependent up-converted photoluminescence property. As seen, the up-converted emission wavelength increases from 348nm to 502nm with the excitation wavelength increases from 550nm to 850nm, it is clearly that the excitation wavelength of 600nm is the optimal excitation value [26]. With above performance, the CDs prepared in pH of 7 were used next for the cell imaging measurements.

Biocompatibility of CDs is one of the most important properties to determine their application in cell-labeling and imaging. To assess the resultant CDs for their potential biomedical imaging applications, S180 sarcoma cells were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the CDs by MTT. Figure 5a shows that the CDs exhibit an extremely low cytotoxicity, with the S180 sarcoma cells retaining a viability of about 98% at the concentration of 20μgmL−1 and 84% at 640μgmL−1, hence it is safe for biological applications. Having established the biocompatibility, we protocol an experiment to demonstrate the potential of CDs for cell imaging by laser scanning confocal microscope. Figure 5b and c show the confocal microscope images of the S180 sarcoma cells incubated with CDs at excitation wavelengths 364nm and 488nm, respectively. The bright blue luminescence in the cell cytoplasm surface region and dark in the cell nucleus are observed at λ ex =364nm, which indicates that the CDs particles were incubate into the living cell. When the laser excitation wavelength is changed to 488nm, a distinct, bright green fluorescent color is observed, which could be ascribed to the excitation dependent fluorescent property of the CDs and shows a great advantage than traditional dye. All these results demonstrated that the CDs derived from sucrose serve as a potential, ideal probe for cell imaging.

In summary, we prepared water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots via a simple-green route with sucrose. The obtained CDs show good monodispersity with an average particle size of 5nm and excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence and up-converted photoluminescence property. Furthermore due to the advantages of high water-solubility, tunable photoluminescence and high biocompatibility, the as-prepared CDs has been successfully applied in cell imaging, which indicate the CDs hold a great potential for the applications in biolabelling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 61006001 and 61176005) and Engagement Program (Program No. 2014046) Funded by Xi’an University of Science and Technology.
